analysis:
- Almost every strong heuristic follows the same 4-stage pattern: nondominated pruning → cheap box/weight scalar scoring → small k‑proportional shortlist → greedy/CELF selection with cached singleton (and sometimes pairwise) HVs → bounded 1-for-1 local swaps and deterministic padding. That structure explains why scores cluster tightly.
- Strengths repeated across the better heuristics: vectorized proxies (rectangular volume, few-weight scalarization) cheaply reduce candidates; CELF-style lazy updates cut redundant HV recomputations; singleton/pairwise caching and explicit HV budgets limit expensive calls; deterministic seeds/tie‑breaks ensure reproducibility.
- Common inefficiencies: many implementations still contain Python-level O(N^2) loops for dominance or membership checks, repeated full-set HV recomputations when incremental updates would suffice, and unnecessary large shortlists that inflate HV cost. Some use list membership checks ("if x in list") where sets would be O(1).
- Missed pruning opportunities: several variants could skip HV evaluations using cheap upper bounds (box-volume or normalized scalar upper bounds) more aggressively; a few compute pairwise HVs even when shortlist is moderately large, wasting calls.
- Local search tradeoffs: bounded swaps help quality, but naive nested loops over selected × remaining candidate sets are expensive; limiting candidate fronts or using priority heuristics for promising swaps reduces HV evaluations.
- Data reuse gaps: many heuristics recompute hv_of(vstack(selected + candidate)) from scratch instead of maintaining incremental structures or reusing union relationships, which would save repeated pg.hypervolume calls.
- Determinism vs randomized shuffles: seeded RNGs are OK for escaping local traps, but deterministic, score-driven tie-breaking is preferable when reproducibility and predictability are required.
experience: Favor a compact, Pareto-aware shortlist (O(k)), CELF lazy greedy with cached singleton HVs and selective pairwise caching, and very aggressive cheap upper‑bound pruning (box- and scalar-based) to avoid HV calls. Use vectorized nondominated filtering, sets/heap for O(1)/O(log n) ops, an HV-call budget, and incremental swaps restricted to top candidates. Keep determinism by score-based tie-breaks; only inject controlled randomness if necessary.
current_reflection: Favor a compact, Pareto-aware shortlist (O(k)), CELF lazy greedy with cached singleton HVs and selective pairwise caching, and very aggressive cheap upper‑bound pruning (box- and scalar-based) to avoid HV calls. Use vectorized nondominated filtering, sets/heap for O(1)/O(log n) ops, an HV-call budget, and incremental swaps restricted to top candidates. Keep determinism by score-based tie-breaks; only inject controlled randomness if necessary.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.
bad_reflections: Favor CELF lazy greedy + singleton (and selective pairwise) caching, adaptive shortlist sized by k+Pareto, strong cheap upper bounds to prune candidates, and incremental cached marginal updates for swaps. Cap HV calls, avoid dense weight sets unless budgeted, and prioritize deterministic tie‑breaks; these give the best quality/cost tradeoff.

Shortlist aggressively but proportionally to k, use CELF with cached singleton HVs and selective pairwise caching, apply cheap upper‑bound pruning (box volumes) before exact HV calls, enforce an HV‑call budget, and do a small bounded swap pass. Reuse arrays, avoid repeated full recomputations, and tune weight/vector counts to the cost‑benefit sweet spot for your problem size.

Make a tight shortlist (k‑proportional), cache singleton HVs and selective pairwise HVs only when shortlist ≤ ~40, use box‑volume upper bounds to skip candidates, run CELF lazy greedy to minimize recomputations, cap HV calls and swaps, avoid costly per‑candidate array copies, and tune weight‑vector count adaptively. Deterministic seeds and set membership for O(1) checks improve reproducibility and speed.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: shortlist O(k), Pareto-aware prefilter, CELF lazy greedy, singleton + selective pairwise HV cache, box/scalar upper bounds, HV-call budget, vectorized nondominated filtering, heaps/sets, bounded incremental swaps, deterministic tie-breaks.
- Advice: prefilter nondominated points, shortlist via approximate HVC with few weight vectors, run CELF using cached singleton HVs and selective pairwise cache, apply aggressive cheap upper bounds to prune, batch HV calls, cap HV budget, then run a small bounded swap pass among top candidates; use deterministic score ties.
- Avoid: full per-candidate HV recomputations, dense weight grids unless budgeted, uncontrolled randomness, heavy Python loops or needless array copies.
- Explanation: minimizes expensive HV evaluations while keeping solution quality high and reproducible; caches, pruning and bounded local search give the best cost/benefit.
